profile_bio.description: • AfricanFantasy Enthusiast • Neurodelightful • 👩🏾‍💻👨🏻‍🔬 👼🏽👧🏽 👧🏽 👶🏽 • She/Her • 🇰🇪🇺🇸x🇳🇱 • Codex

media.ext_alt_text: Black graphic with stylized red line art of a castle labeled “The Liitokho (The Lady Eshiala’s Seat).” White text reads “Shortlisted by 3 editors RevPit 2026.” Title: “An Agent’s Guide to Stonebound,” with “Stonebound” in large yellow letters. Subtitle: “an Adult Folkloric High Fantasy Novel by Anindo Zelle,” ~120K words. Tags: African Fantasy, Kenyan Folklore, Mythological Retelling, and “Star-Crossed Lovers Subplot.” Decorative stars, a ringed planet, and dotted footsteps accent the design.

Black infographic-style graphic outlining a fantasy plot. Teal portrait of Sadaka wa Wafula labeled “Eshiala’s ‘token’ leader,” who leads her people under Winam’s occupation and keeps secrets. A note reads “Winam’s ancient Stone King dies,” beside a crowned skull labeled “Not immortal after all.” Red illustration of a fortress labeled “The Odrodh (The Stone King’s Palace).” Text states “Eshiala’s survival demands the unthinkable—marriage to the enemy,” shown with a contract icon. Yellow portrait of Sidhokano wud Mirimalit, “Winam’s youngest Stone Prince,” described as having a “grumpy inner beast.” Dotted paths connect elements with phrases like “The fragile peace shatters” and “The two have history.”

Black infographic-style graphic continuing a fantasy plot. A cloaked figure labeled “Has a covert mission.” Text explains Sadaka searches the Odrodh for a key to Eshiala’s liberation. A couple icon notes “He falls first (but she’s not too far behind)” and that he’s “not the monster she expected.” A key icon marks her discovering how to destroy his kind, though her goals shift. Red text: “Meanwhile… a sinister faith hunts wielders” and “The gods are determined to tear Sadaka & Sidhokano apart,” shown with a temple illustration. Final line: she must choose between betraying him for her people or protecting him and risking both nations.

Black infographic titled “COMPS” and “SNIPPET” in white chalk-style font. Four comp titles with covers and themes: Masquerade (court intrigue, myth retelling), The Final Strife (politics, African mythology), The House of Rust (Kenyan-inspired), and The Broken Earth Trilogy (geologic magic, survival). A teal box shows a Chapter 1 excerpt: “SADAKA. When it came to any body of water larger than her bathing pool at the Liitokho in Mumia, Sadaka wa Wafula tried to follow one rule. Don’t go in. Especially not when facing unknown depths.” Teal stars, a location pin, and dotted footprints decorate the layout.

kept.text:
This guy Ryan heard Nikita Beir say original videos were fine… but if you really wanted those mythical six-point video ratings, you had to go live.

That was all the motivation he needed.

So instead of posting a normal clip like a sane person, this guy fired up a livestream and marched straight into his backyard like he was unveiling the eighth wonder of the world. He starts panning across his garden bed—fresh plants, decorative rocks, stone borders, blooming white service berry tree, the whole suburban masterpiec, like he’s hosting an HGTV finale.

Meanwhile the livestream has three viewers:
his mom, one random bot account, and someone asking if this is a funeral.

Ryan ignores all of it because in his mind every flower bed angle is stacking algorithm points. He zooms in on the blue birdbath like it’s premium content. At one point he actually says, “this is six-point material right here.”

The funniest part is he spent weeks landscaping the yard… and somehow cared more about Nikita’s scoring system than the actual garden.

Now the big question:

does livestreaming your backyard count as genius-level strategy… or did Ryan basically invent QVC for mulch? 🌱📱
